title: Why Unit Conversion is Multiplying by 1

md: To convert $3$ feet into inches, you multiply by a **conversion factor**:
But hold on — multiplying changes a number. How can $3$ "become" $36$ without changing the actual length? The resolution: that conversion factor is secretly equal to **$1$**.

blocks.title: The claim
blocks.body: Every conversion factor, like $\dfrac{12\text{ in}}{1\text{ ft}}$, equals the number $1$ — so multiplying by it never changes the quantity, only its units.

md: ### Step 1 — A conversion fact is an equation
"$12$ inches make a foot" isn't a fraction — it's a statement that two lengths are **the same**:
### Step 2 — Divide both sides by the same thing
Take that equation and divide both sides by $1\text{ ft}$. Whatever you do to one side of a true equation, you may do to the other:
The conversion factor literally **equals $1$**. (Divide the other way and you get $\dfrac{1\text{ ft}}{12\text{ in}} = 1$ too — both directions are $1$.)
So when you write $3\text{ ft} \times \dfrac{12\text{ in}}{1\text{ ft}}$, you are multiplying by $1$. The *amount* of length is untouched — exactly as $5 \times 1 = 5$.
What the factor *does* change is the **bookkeeping**: the "ft" on top of $3\text{ ft}$ cancels the "ft" on the bottom of the factor, the way identical numbers cancel in a fraction:

blocks.title: The practical rule
blocks.body: Always write the conversion factor with the **old unit on the bottom**, so it cancels — and the **new unit on top**, so it survives. Pick that orientation and the units do the thinking for you. $\blacksquare$
